<findings>

[observation] [warning] Cohesion of 0.46 is borderline — if new utilities are added here without a clear shared-foundation rationale they will erode cohesion further; any utility used by only one zone should live in that zone instead.
[observation] [info] Coupling of 0.54 is driven entirely by inbound imports from consumer zones, not outbound leakage — a healthy signal that the foundation pattern is working correctly.
[observation] [info] The single test file (node-culler.test.ts) co-locates well with its production peer; the other two types files (data-files.ts, view-id.ts) are untested — consider coverage if these types carry validation logic.
[pattern] [info] web-shared's 0.46 coupling is structurally healthy — it is purely consumed (3 consumer zones, 0 dependencies) and its below-threshold cohesion reflects the natural diversity of a shared utilities layer rather than a cohesion problem; files added here should continue to be audited for genuine framework-agnosticism
[relationship] [info] web-shared correctly receives only inbound imports (never exports to a higher tier) from both production zones and web-unit — this one-way inbound pattern is the canonical healthy signal for a foundation layer and should be preserved as the zone grows.
[anti-pattern] [warning] node-culler.ts in web-shared has zero importers — it is dead code in the web package's foundation layer. Remove or relocate it: dead files in a foundation layer mislead contributors into treating the utility as maintained infrastructure and artificially suppress the zone's cohesion metric.
[anti-pattern] [warning] packages/web/src/viewer/crash/crash-detector.ts imports ViewId via a direct sub-module path (../../shared/view-id.js) rather than through the web-shared barrel, bypassing the barrel's auditable re-export surface — this inconsistency makes it impossible to determine all ViewId consumers by inspecting the barrel's import graph alone.
[anti-pattern] [info] web-shared's index.ts barrel mixes a stateful runtime class export (NodeCuller) with pure type and constant re-exports (ViewId, DATA_FILES) — a consumer importing only `ViewId` for type-checking purposes is semantically coupled to the NodeCuller module, preventing reliable dead-code elimination and obscuring the distinction between type-only and runtime dependencies on this zone.
[suggestion] [warning] Removing the confirmed-dead node-culler.ts from web-shared would reduce the zone from 5 to 4 files and is the single highest-leverage action to restore the zone's cohesion metric above the 0.5 warning threshold. No structural refactoring is required — deletion alone resolves the threshold alert and eliminates the misleading signal that NodeCuller is maintained infrastructure.
[suggestion] [warning] node-culler.ts's docstring references 'CulledNode component (prd-tree.ts)' as its consumer, but prd-tree.ts has no NodeCuller import and listener-lifecycle.ts only cites it in a @see JSDoc. The stale docstring is the most likely reason previous contributors did not remove node-culler.ts when its actual consumer was removed or refactored away — the comment signals active use where none exists. Update or remove the docstring alongside the dead file removal.
[suggestion] [warning] Delete packages/web/src/shared/node-culler.ts and remove its export from the web-shared barrel (packages/web/src/shared/index.ts). Also update the stale @see JSDoc in listener-lifecycle.ts that references node-culler.ts. This single deletion restores cohesion above the 0.5 warning threshold, eliminates the misleading infrastructure signal, and resolves the stale docstring confusion — the highest leverage-per-action change in this zone.
[suggestion] [warning] Update packages/web/src/viewer/crash/crash-detector.ts to import ViewId through the web-shared barrel (e.g. ../../shared/index.js) instead of the current direct submodule path (../../shared/view-id.js). This is the minimal fix corroborated by both web-shared finding #6 and global finding #11: restoring the auditable import surface requires only a one-line import path change.

</findings>

<insights>

- This zone is the canonical zero-upward-dependency foundation for the web package — boundary-check.test.ts enforces that nothing here imports from viewer or server zones.
- Moderate cohesion (0.46) is expected for a utility layer that intentionally groups heterogeneous helpers; watch for growth that would dilute this further.
- Three distinct consumer zones (web-viewer, crash, web-unit) import from here, confirming its role as a genuine shared foundation rather than a convenience grab-bag.
- Cohesion of 0.46 is borderline — if new utilities are added here without a clear shared-foundation rationale they will erode cohesion further; any utility used by only one zone should live in that zone instead.
- The single test file (node-culler.test.ts) co-locates well with its production peer; the other two types files (data-files.ts, view-id.ts) are untested — consider coverage if these types carry validation logic.
- Coupling of 0.54 is driven entirely by inbound imports from consumer zones, not outbound leakage — a healthy signal that the foundation pattern is working correctly.
- web-shared receives inbound imports from 3 distinct zones (web-viewer with 5, web-unit with 1, crash with 1) but has zero outbound cross-zone imports — its coupling score (0.46) is entirely driven by consumer fan-in, confirming it is a well-behaved foundation layer with no upward leakage
- web-shared's 0.46 coupling is structurally healthy — it is purely consumed (3 consumer zones, 0 dependencies) and its below-threshold cohesion reflects the natural diversity of a shared utilities layer rather than a cohesion problem; files added here should continue to be audited for genuine framework-agnosticism
- web-shared correctly receives only inbound imports (never exports to a higher tier) from both production zones and web-unit — this one-way inbound pattern is the canonical healthy signal for a foundation layer and should be preserved as the zone grows.
- node-culler.ts in web-shared has zero importers across the entire web package — it is dead code residing in the foundation layer. Dead code in a foundation layer inflates the apparent API surface and suppresses the zone's reported cohesion metric by adding a file with no import graph edges.
- The three active files in web-shared serve distinct consumer sets: data-files.ts is consumed by both server routes (directly) and viewer (via external.ts gateway); view-id.ts is consumed by crash/ and viewer (via external.ts). node-culler.ts contributes no edges. The effective cohesion of web-shared's active files is higher than the reported 0.46 — the low metric is partly an artifact of the unused file inflating the denominator.
- node-culler.ts in web-shared has zero importers — it is dead code in the web package's foundation layer. Remove or relocate it: dead files in a foundation layer mislead contributors into treating the utility as maintained infrastructure and artificially suppress the zone's cohesion metric.
- web-shared's index.ts barrel mixes a stateful runtime class export (NodeCuller) with pure type and constant re-exports (ViewId, DATA_FILES) — a consumer importing only `ViewId` for type-checking purposes is semantically coupled to the NodeCuller module, preventing reliable dead-code elimination and obscuring the distinction between type-only and runtime dependencies on this zone.
- packages/web/src/viewer/crash/crash-detector.ts imports ViewId via a direct sub-module path (../../shared/view-id.js) rather than through the web-shared barrel, bypassing the barrel's auditable re-export surface — this inconsistency makes it impossible to determine all ViewId consumers by inspecting the barrel's import graph alone.
- node-culler.ts contains a docstring claiming 'Preact integration is handled in the CulledNode component (prd-tree.ts)' but prd-tree.ts contains no import of NodeCuller, and listener-lifecycle.ts in the same prd-tree/ directory references node-culler.ts only via a @see JSDoc comment — not an import. The docstring is stale documentation from a planned or removed integration, creating a false impression that NodeCuller is actively maintained infrastructure with a known consumer.
- The 33 internal call-graph edges for web-shared are entirely attributable to NodeCuller's internal method structure (constructor → observer.observe, handleEntries → callbacks.get/heights.set/callback, observe → callbacks.set, dispose → observer.disconnect, etc.) — all within a single file. The call-graph cohesion of 1.0 reflects intra-file method density rather than cross-file zone coordination, making it a misleading metric for a zone with 4 distinct source files.
- node-culler.ts's docstring references 'CulledNode component (prd-tree.ts)' as its consumer, but prd-tree.ts has no NodeCuller import and listener-lifecycle.ts only cites it in a @see JSDoc. The stale docstring is the most likely reason previous contributors did not remove node-culler.ts when its actual consumer was removed or refactored away — the comment signals active use where none exists. Update or remove the docstring alongside the dead file removal.
- Removing the confirmed-dead node-culler.ts from web-shared would reduce the zone from 5 to 4 files and is the single highest-leverage action to restore the zone's cohesion metric above the 0.5 warning threshold. No structural refactoring is required — deletion alone resolves the threshold alert and eliminates the misleading signal that NodeCuller is maintained infrastructure.
- Finding #6 (crash-detector.ts direct import of view-id classified as anti-pattern) directly contradicts global finding #8, which documents the direct import as intentional for cycle prevention via performance/index.ts re-exports. The anti-pattern classification is incorrect — this is a deliberate exception with a documented structural rationale. Severity corrected to info.
- Findings #5, #8, and #9 form a tight corroboration cluster: node-culler.ts has zero importers, a stale docstring citing a non-existent consumer, and its presence directly suppresses zone cohesion below the 0.5 warning threshold. These three findings share a single root cause and a single fix — deletion alone resolves all three simultaneously.
- Delete packages/web/src/shared/node-culler.ts and remove its export from the web-shared barrel (packages/web/src/shared/index.ts). Also update the stale @see JSDoc in listener-lifecycle.ts that references node-culler.ts. This single deletion restores cohesion above the 0.5 warning threshold, eliminates the misleading infrastructure signal, and resolves the stale docstring confusion — the highest leverage-per-action change in this zone.
- Update packages/web/src/viewer/crash/crash-detector.ts to import ViewId through the web-shared barrel (e.g. ../../shared/index.js) instead of the current direct submodule path (../../shared/view-id.js). This is the minimal fix corroborated by both web-shared finding #6 and global finding #11: restoring the auditable import surface requires only a one-line import path change.
- Four independent findings across two analysis passes (#5 automated, #8 LLM pass 4, #9 LLM pass 4, #10 LLM pass 5) all converge on node-culler.ts as confirmed dead code. This is the highest-confidence finding in the zone and the single highest-leverage deletion available: removing packages/web/src/shared/node-culler.ts and its export from packages/web/src/shared/index.ts simultaneously restores cohesion above the 0.5 threshold, eliminates the stale infrastructure signal, and cleans the @see JSDoc in listener-lifecycle.ts. No other action in this zone has comparable evidence density.
- Finding #6 (crash-detector.ts barrel bypass) and global finding #15 (performance/index.ts zone-crossing barrel) are causally linked. Fixing crash-detector.ts alone does not restore the auditable web-shared import surface unless performance/index.ts's cross-zone re-exports are removed first. These two actions must be sequenced: performance fix first, then crash-detector.ts fix.
